reasoning: The latest SOFR data shows it dropping to 3.57% in a Fed target range of 3.50-3.75%. This is relatively low and subject to upward mean reversion in the very short term (e.g., April 19 and May 12), particularly with mid-April tax day liquidity drains historically putting upward pressure on SOFR. Therefore, short horizon probabilities are above 0.5. However, there is broad consensus (as seen in the March 2026 dot plot and CME FedWatch) for a 25 basis point rate cut later in 2026, which would push SOFR below 3.57. Thus, for horizons going into late 2026 and early 2027, the probability of an increase drops significantly. Further out (2029-2036), forecasting the exact stage of the macroeconomic rate cycle involves much greater uncertainty, so probabilities progressively revert back toward 0.5, adhering to the instruction that distant horizon predictions should express maximum uncertainty.
